title: India's Russian Oil Imports Set to Hit All-Time High, Even as Iran Crude Comeback Looms
summary: India's June crude imports from Russia are on track for a record 2.35 million barrels per day — over 53% of total crude intake — as the Strait of Hormuz disruption reshapes supply chains. The record comes in the same week the US sanctions waiver on Russian oil expired, adding fresh uncertainty just as the first round of US-Iran nuclear talks raises the prospect of Iranian crude re-entering global markets.

coverages.headline: India's Russian oil imports set to hit all-time high this month, ahead of Iran crude comeback
coverages.excerpt: India's June crude imports from Russia are on track for a record 2.35 million bpd — over 53% of total crude intake — as the Strait of Hormuz disruption reshapes supply; the US sanctions waiver on Russian oil also expired this week, adding uncertainty.
coverages.bias_notes: The juxtaposition in the headline — a Russian import record arriving 'ahead of' a potential Iran crude comeback — correctly frames this as a transitional, possibly short-lived peak rather than a stable new normal, which is the right level of caution for a supply chain reshaped by an active geopolitical crisis. The expiry of the US sanctions waiver on Russian oil is reported as adding 'uncertainty' without specifying what concrete consequence that expiry has — whether it means new penalties, restricted payment channels, or simply a more contested diplomatic environment for India's continued purchases. At 53% of total crude intake, India's energy security is now more concentrated in a single supplier relationship than at almost any point in recent years, a dependency risk that gets less attention than the immediate cost-saving benefits of discounted Russian crude.
